coredata.dc:title: LCQDs modified Bi2MoO6 hydrangea composite photocatalyst for tetracycline degradation from wastewater: Performance, efficiency, and adsorption synergistic photocatalytic mechanism

coredata.dc:description:
This study used alkaline lignin as the carbon source for preparing alkaline lignin carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) based on the solvothermal method. To achieve efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracyclic hydrochloride antibiotics (TCH), hydrangea-shaped LCQDs/Bi2MoO6 composites were prepared in situ by the solvothermal method exploiting the optical properties of LCQDs. The composite material was systematically characterized using XRD, FTIR, XPS, SEM, TEM, EIS, nitrogen adsorption-desorption testing, and UV–vis DRS to determine its crystal structure, microstructure, and optoelectronic properties. The results show that the sample has high purity and good crystallinity. The experiment on photocatalytic degradation further demonstrated that the LCQDs/Bi2MoO6 composite material modified with LCQDs exhibited superior photocatalytic activity compared to a single Bi2MoO6. Among them, BMO/LCQDs-7 showed the best effect. After 120 min of illumination, the rate of TCH removal through photocatalysis was 98.76%, with a degradation rate constant: K= 0.026 min−1. After five cycles, the removal rate of TCH can still be maintained at 79.35%. The photocatalytic degradation of TCH has been demonstrated through EPR and LC-MS experiments primarily caused by h+ and ·O2
- .

head.ce:title: LCQDs modified Bi2MoO6 hydrangea composite photocatalyst for tetracycline degradation from wastewater: Performance, efficiency, and adsorption synergistic photocatalytic mechanism

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
This study used alkaline lignin as the carbon source for preparing alkaline lignin carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) based on the solvothermal method. To achieve efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracyclic hydrochloride antibiotics (TCH), hydrangea-shaped LCQDs/Bi2MoO6 composites were prepared in situ by the solvothermal method exploiting the optical properties of LCQDs. The composite material was systematically characterized using XRD, FTIR, XPS, SEM, TEM, EIS, nitrogen adsorption-desorption testing, and UV–vis DRS to determine its crystal structure, microstructure, and optoelectronic properties. The results show that the sample has high purity and good crystallinity. The experiment on photocatalytic degradation further demonstrated that the LCQDs/Bi2MoO6 composite material modified with LCQDs exhibited superior photocatalytic activity compared to a single Bi2MoO6. Among them, BMO/LCQDs-7 showed the best effect. After 120 min of illumination, the rate of TCH removal through photocatalysis was 98.76%, with a degradation rate constant: K= 0.026 min−1. After five cycles, the removal rate of TCH can still be maintained at 79.35%. The photocatalytic degradation of TCH has been demonstrated through EPR and LC-MS experiments primarily caused by h+ and ·O2
- .

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is the most common antibiotic drug in the world. Due to its low production price and significant therapeutic effects, it has a wide range of applications in human therapeutics, aquaculture, and animal husbandry [1,2]. Despite the multiple benefits of TC for human and animal health, only a tiny fraction of TC can be metabolized or absorbed in the body. In contrast, up to 75% of TC is released into the environment through human or animal excreta [3], and it is difficult to degrade TC accumulation in ecosystems and generates TC resistance genes in bacterial populations [4]. With the continuous spread of TC resistance genes in the environment, it leads to the acquisition of drug resistance by pathogenic bacteria, thus posing a potential hazard to the ecosystem and public health [5]. Therefore, it is imperative to treat TC in wastewater. Currently, several methods for degrading TCH have been reported, such as advanced oxidation processes [6], biological method [7], membrane treatment [8], adsorption method [9]. However, conventional methods are inefficient in removing TCH from wastewater. Photocatalysis, as one of the most promising means of environmental remediation, has been widely researched and developed in recent years for its advantages of high efficiency, economy, and greenness, especially in the field of photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants in wastewater [10,11]. Therefore, the development of a low-cost, green and efficient photocatalyst is the key to this technology.

Currently, there are various catalysts available in the field of photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, such as TiO2 [12], g-C3N4 [13], and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [14]. Wherein, Bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6) is a typical Aurivillius oxide, with excellent ferroelectric properties. Meanwhile, its two-dimensional layered structure mainly comprises alternating layers of (Bi2O2)2+ and (MoO4)2-. Consequently, the spontaneous polarization electric field inside is essential for improving photocatalytic efficiency [15,16]. Furthermore, Bi2MoO6 possesses an inherent semiconductor characteristic with a bandgap width of 2.5–3.2 eV, enabling it to efficiently capture visible light and maintain strong photocatalytic oxidation-reduction abilities efficiently [17]. However, the low rate of electron transfer and high rate of electron-hole recombination in a single Bi2MoO6 semiconductor limit its application in the field of photocatalysis [18]. Modification is necessary to improve further the photocatalytic activity of Bi2MoO6 semiconductor materials [19]. In recent years, researchers have proposed various modification strategies, such as morphology modification [20], constructing heterojunctions [21,22], elemental doping [23], and metal ion defect engineering [24], to improve the photocatalytic activity of Bi2MoO6. Biochar composite semiconductor materials are an economical and effective way to enhance photocatalytic capability. Xing et al. synthesized carbon-doped lamellar Bi2MoO6 nanomaterials using glucose as a carbon source through hydrothermal-calcination. And the carbon element substituted the host O2- anions in the lattice of Bi2MoO6, which induced the lattice expansion, and band structure variation [25]. Xu et al. further promoted the photodegradation of TC by loading Bi2MoO6-MOF on the biopolymer bacterial cellulose (BC), utilizing the hydroxyl group in BC to act as a hole trap [26]. Meanwhile, Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts have various morphologies, including nanosheets [27], nanotubes [28], and nanospheres [29]. Particularly, the hydrangea-shaped Bi2MoO6 consists of 2D flakes that cross each other and cluster together to form a 3D flower-like structure. This spherical structure has better crystallinity and more compact grains compared to the lamellar structure, which increases the reaction contact area as well as the reflectivity to light, thus increasing the photocatalytic activity [30].

Lignin is the largest natural aromatic polymer in nature. It has a complex chemical structure, is difficult to degrade, and has low high-value utilization efficiency [31]. Therefore, preparing renewable and cost-effective carbon quantum from depolymerized alkali lignin has good development prospects. Alkaline lignin carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with a particle size of less than 10 nm, prepared using alkaline lignin as a carbon source. Compared to large particle biochar, LCQDs have gained significant attention due to their excellent fluorescence, low cost, green and non-toxic properties, and good water solubility [32] Concurrently, considering the well-controlled electron transport and storage capabilities of LCQDs, and the excellent up-converted property, they can be used to improve the semiconductor photocatalytic activity [33]. According to the previously reported works, the methods for the preparation of CQDs have received extensive attention [34], including solvothermal [35], microwave-assisted [36], ultrasonic-assisted [37], laser ablation [38], and electric arc discharge [39]. However, some of the methods have the problems of high equipment cost, harsh synthesis conditions, and cumbersome processes. In contrast, the solvothermal method is a non-toxic, low-cost, easy-to-operate with high fluorescence quantum yield [40,41]. Solvothermal technology is under high temperature and high-pressure conditions, using organic matter or non-aqueous solvents as solvents to make the precursors that can not be reacted at room temperature and normal pressure to be fully dissolved in this reaction system and reach a certain degree of supersaturation. Thereby, atomic or molecular growth motifs are formed, and uniform and well-crystallized nanoscale powder particles are obtained after nucleation and crystallization. Further more, the resulting material is homogeneous in composition, high in purity, and has a controlled particle morphology. At the same time, the high-pressure closed system makes the solvothermal technology environmentally friendly and economical [42].

However, up to now, reports have not been found on preparing this novel LCQDs/Bi2MoO6 (BMO/LCQDs) hydrangea-like composite in photodegradable TC. Thus, in this work, we report for the first time the successful synthesis of a novel hydrangea-like BMO/LCQDs composite photocatalyst through a simple solvothermal and in situ co-precipitation method. The photocatalytic activity of the obtained BMO/LCQDs composites was evaluated by removing tetracycline hydrochloride from wastewater under visible light irradiation. Compared with other reported catalysts (Table S1), this study accelerated the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers by using cheap and easily available LCQDs as electron reservoirs, effectively solving the problems of difficult electron-hole separation and high compounding rate of a single Bi2MoO6 semiconductor material, and realized the high-efficiency degradation of TCH in a short time. Additionally, the ideal mass ratio of LCQDs to Bi2MoO6 has been determined. Next, various characterization techniques were used to study the crystal structure, morphology, band structure, and optoelectronic properties of BMO/LCQDs composite materials, confirming the successful construction of the composites. Finally, the intermediates were detected based on LC-MS, and the degradation pathway of TCH was proposed to provide a new strategy for photocatalytic treatment of water pollution.

Tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) purchased from Biotoped (Beijing, China); Alkaline lignin (AL); Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW58000); Benzoquinone (BQ) was purchased from McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); Ethylene diamine (EDA); Sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4·2 H2O); Bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O) purchased from Chemical Reagent Factory 4 (Tianjin, China); Isopropanol (IPA); Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA); Anhydrous ethanol (EtOH); Ethylene glycol (EG); Purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents are analytical pure, and the water used in the experiment is deionized water (DI), which does not require further purification during use.

According to the literature [43]. At the same time, considering that the vibration of CC and CO bonds in the surface groups of CQDs prepared by the alcohol thermal method is more obvious and more fluorescent (Fig. S1) [44]. Therefore, in this study, anhydrous ethanol was used to replace the original deionized water as the base solvent, and solvothermal technique was used to prepare the obtained LCQDs. Fig. 1a shows the addition of alkaline lignin (1 g) to anhydrous ethanol (94 mL), followed by sonication for 30 min. Next, 6 mL of EDA is added to the mixture and stirred continuously for 30 min. Transfer the suspension to a 150 mL high-pressure reactor lined with PTFE and stainless steel, heat it to 180℃, and maintain it for 12 h. After the reaction, cool the reactor to room temperature to obtain an unpurified solution of LCQDs. The solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 48 h. In addition, large particulate carbon was removed with 0.22 μm microporous organic filter membrane, that is difficult to disperse in the dialysis solution. Finally, purified LCQDs solution was obtained.

According to literature [30] synthesized Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst, furthermore, in this study, novel hydrangea-like BMO/LCQDs composites with different ratios of LCQDs doping were prepared for the first time by in situ co-precipitation technique. As shown in Fig. 1b, add Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O (4 mmol) and Na2MoO4·2 H2O (2 mmol) to 30 mL ethylene glycol and stir until completely dissolved to give solutions A and B; Then, slowly add solution B to solution A. Weigh PVP (0.3 g) as a spherical crystal growth regulator, add it to the AB mixture, stir continuously for 30 min, then add different ratios of LCQDs solution (4 mL, 7 mL, 10 mL) to the mixture, and continue stirring for 1 h until the mixture is homogeneous. After that, transfer the colloidal suspension to a 150 mL high-pressure reactor and heat it to 160℃, maintaining this temperature for 12 h. After completion of the reaction, cool it to room temperature, then filter the mixture and wash it several times with anhydrous ethanol. The composite material is obtained by vacuum drying the sample at 80℃ for 8 h. Finally, Bi2MoO6 monomer and novel BMO/LCQDs (BMO/LCQDs-4, BMO/LCQDs-7, BMO/LCQDs-10) composites were obtained (Fig. S2).

The Rigaku D/MAX-3B X-ray diffractometer (XRD) and Shimadzu AXIS UltraDLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) from Japan were used to characterize the structure and elemental valence states of the samples. The surface morphology and element distribution of photocatalysts were observed using FEISirion-200 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and JEM-2010 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) manufactured by Japan Electronics Co., Ltd. Analyse the specific surface area of the sample using the N2 adsorption-desorption test (BET). The FTIR infrared spectrum was obtained using the Nicolet is50 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer from China Thermo Fisher Scientific Technology Co., Ltd. Toshiba Corporation's USB4000 UV visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) was used in Japan to determine the optical and physical properties of the sample. The electrochemical performance was analyzed by photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Mott-Schottky spectroscopy (MS), and Transient photocurrent (TPC) using a VMPS electrochemical workstation. The A300–10–12 EPR electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR) from Brooke, Germany was used to analyze the free radicals in the system. The aim of this study is to identify the products and degradation pathways resulting from the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride, using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS).

This study aims to evaluate the photocatalytic degradation performance of a catalyst using TCH as the target pollutant. Firstly, 20 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed into a photocatalytic reactor containing 100 mL of TCH (20 mg/L, initial pH=6) solution. And the system was magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 min to reach adsorption equilibrium. Under visible light (300 W xenon lamp with λ>420 nm filter) irradiation, 5 mL was sampled at 30 min intervals and filtered using a 0.22 μm filter to separate the catalyst [45]. The filtrate was measured and analysed in a UV–visible spectrophotometer (λ=363 nm) [46–49]. Each experiment was performed in triplicate. The degradation rate of TCH was calculated according to the following Eq. (A.1):
(A.1)

where η is the degradation rate of TCH, %; A0 and At represent initial absorbance and absorbance after degradation of TCH; C0 and Ct represent initial concentration and concentration after degradation of TCH.

The effects of other experimental parameters, such as pH, bursting agent capture, and catalyst dosing, on the photocatalytic effect of BMO/LCQDs were investigated. Meanwhile, the suspension after the reaction was centrifuged, filtered and washed, and then dried at 80 °C for 8 h in a vacuum drying oven to obtain the recycled photocatalyst powder, which was subjected to cyclic degradation experiments under the same environmental conditions.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to analyse the phase composition and crystal structure of single Bi2MoO6 and BMO/LCQDs composites (BMO/LCQDs-4, BMO/LCQDs-7, and BMO/LCQDs-10). Fig. 2a shows the Bi2MoO6 composites doped with different proportions of LCQDs. Observed diffraction peaks at 2θ=28.30°, 32.50°, 32.60°, 47.20°, 55.60° are ascribed to the facets of (131), (200), (002), (062), (133) crystal planes of Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS No. 72–1524) [30,50], respectively. The above diffraction peak was not shifted, indicating the successful synthesis of Bi2MoO6 and its corresponding composites. At the same time, no additional diffraction peaks were detected, further confirming the sample's purity. And the sample's sharp diffraction peaks for each crystal plane indicate good crystallinity. In addition, the diffraction peak pattern of BMO/LCQDs composites is identical to that of single Bi2MoO6, indicating that the loading of LCQDs does not alter the original crystal structure of Bi2MoO6. Furthermore, no significant diffraction peaks of LCQDs were observed. This is primarily due to the low content, higher dispersion, and lower crystallinity of the introduced LCQDs.

The surface groups of the synthesized photocatalysts were analyzed using fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Fig. 2b shows the stretching vibration of Mo-O bonds and the asymmetric bending vibration of Mo-O-Mo bonds, causing the two peaks at 730.90 and 840.80 cm−1. And the absorption peak observed at 563.10 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the Bi-O bond. Meanwhile, a peak at 1631.50 cm−1 was observed, indicating the contraction vibration of the O-H bond in the sample [51]. In addition, the peak patterns of the composite and single Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts in the FTIR spectrum were highly consistent. This suggests that the surface groups of the Bi2MoO6 samples were not affected by the doping of LCQDs. However, the peaks occurring at 1200–1500 cm−1 are mainly due to the stretching pattern of the sample's C-N heterocycles of the triazine ring [52]. It can be clearly observed that the peak shape of the composite sample is sharper and the peak area is larger, which marks the success of the construction of the BMO/LCQDs composites.

Fig. 3 shows that scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to study the morphology and micro-nano structures of Bi2MoO6, BMO/LCQDs-4, BMO/LCQDs-7, and BMO/LCQDs-10. In Fig. 3a, the pure Bi2MoO6 exhibits an irregular hydrangea-like morphology consisting of aggregated and intersected nanosheets, and the average particle size of microspheres is about 1 μm, and the particles are stacked with each other. Fig. 3b-d shows that there is almost no change in morphology after loading different ratios of LCQDs on Bi2MoO6. This is mainly because the particle size of LCQDs is less than 10 nm, which cannot be directly observed by SEM, so TEM was used to further analyse the surface of the composite catalysts. In Fig. 3e, the LCQDs particles can be observed, with the LCQDs particle size of 2 nm and the lattice spacing of about 0.21 nm. Fig. 3f-h shows that in the BMO/LCQDs composites, the LCQDs particles are uniformly dispersed on the surface of the Bi2MoO6 microspheres without altering the original structure of Bi2MoO6. The above results indicate the successful preparation of binary BMO/LCQDs composite materials. In addition, the EDS elemental images of BMO/LCQDs-7 further confirmed the presence of Bi, Mo, and O elements in the prepared samples, which were uniformly dispersed, indicating that the binary BMO/LCQDs composites were successfully prepared. Meanwhile, the elemental distribution of BMO/LCQDs showed that the content of O element was more dominant, and the Bi and Mo elements were uniformly dispersed on the surface of the samples with comparable contents (Fig. 3i-l).

With the doping of LCQDs, the specific surface area (BET) and pore volume of the composites were changed. In Fig. 4a, the specific surface areas of Bi2MoO6 and BMO/LCQDs-7 were measured by the N2 adsorption-desorption technique. According to the IUPAC classification, all the prepared samples belong to type II adsorption isotherms with hysteresis loops. The hysteresis loop appeared in the P/P0 region from 0.8 to 1.0, which was mainly attributed to the formation of slit mesopores by the aggregation of Bi2MoO6 and LCQDs with each other [53]. The specific surface areas of Bi2MoO6 and BMO/LCQDs-7 were 25.91 and 35.93 m2/g, respectively. Generally, the higher the specific surface area of a catalyst, the higher the adsorption capacity and the better the photocatalytic performance [54]. Therefore, the doping of LCQDs can not only increase the specific surface area but also increase the pore size, so that the surface of the microspheres has more abundant active sites, which improves the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst.

It is known that PL emission of a semiconductor is mainly attributed to the recombination of charge carrier, so PL could be employed to study the transfer and separation efficiency of photoelectron-hole pairs in semiconductors [13,55]. Therefore, the lower the PL intensity, the higher the electron-hole separation efficiency. In Fig. 4b, the luminescence peaks of the samples were mainly concentrated at 445 nm. The PL intensity of BMO/LCQDs is lower than that of pure Bi2MoO6, indicating that the doping of LCQDs effectively hinders the photogenerated carrier complexation. Meanwhile, the BMO/LCQDs-7 peak intensity was the lowest, which indicated that the appropriate amount of LCQDs promoted the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers in the system, resulting in the significant enhancement of the photocatalytic activity of BMO/LCQDs-7.

In Fig. 4c, the magnitude of transient photocurrent response densities of the prepared samples was in the following order: BMO/LCQDs-7> BMO/LCQDs-10> BMO/LCQDs-4> Bi2MoO6. Theoretically, the higher the photocurrent response densities of the samples, the higher the photocatalytic activity [56]. Among them, the main reason for the high photocurrent response intensity of BMO/LCQDs-7 is the introduction of a moderate amount of LCQDs, which act as charge carriers due to their special conjugated π-structure, accelerating the photogenerated carrier migration and hindering the electron-hole pair complexation. However, an excessive amount of LCQDs not only results in charge overloading, but also affects the absorption of light by Bi2MoO6, reduces e- generation, and affects the photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic activity of a photocatalyst is determined by its light absorption capacity. Fig. 5 shows the absorption characteristics of Bi2MoO6, BMO/LCQDs-4, BMO/LCQDs-7, and BMO/LCQDs-10 as analyzed by ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS). In Fig. 5a, the sample exhibits good absorption performance in both the ultraviolet and visible regions, indicating its potential as a photocatalytic material. The absorption wavelength of pure Bi2MoO6 is approximately 345 nm. When LCQDs are added, the light absorption range of BMO/LCQDs composites is redshifted. Of all the observed effects, the red shift effect of BMO/LCQDs-7 was the most prominent, resulting in an extension of the visible light absorption range to 401 nm. The results suggest that including LCQDs can enhance the light capture capability of Bi2MoO6, improving the composites' photocatalytic activity. The presence of spontaneously polarized electric fields in the Bi2MoO6 and BMO/LCQDs composites causes band gap migration, resulting in steep and tortuous light absorption curves. Additionally, the band gap of the samples was calculated using the Kubelka-Munk formula and then converted using the Tauc formula (Eq. (B.1)) [57]:
(B.1)
a h v = A
( h v − E g )
n / 2
Where a is the diffuse light absorption coefficient; h is Planck's constant; v is the frequency of light; A is the semiconductor material constant; and Eg is the forbidden bandwidth; The type of semiconductor determines the value of n. For direct semiconductors, n equals 1; while for indirect semiconductors, n equals 4 [58]. Bi2MoO6 is a direct bandgap semiconductor with an n-value of 4 [59]. In Fig. 5b, the Eg values of Bi2MoO6, BMO/LCQDs-4, BMO/LCQDs-7, and BMO/LCQDs-10 were 3.12 eV, 2.99 eV, 2.73 eV and 2.89 eV, respectively. The findings are comparable to those previously documented [60,61].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a useful technique for measuring the carrier transport rate in photocatalysts. Generally, the radius of the curve indicates the rate of charge transfer. A smaller radius in the spectrum implies less resistance and more efficient charge migration [62]. Fig. 6a displays the EIS plots for Bi2MoO6, BMO/LCQDs-4, BMO/LCQDs-7, and BMO/LCQDs-10. The composite BMO/LCQDs have a significantly smaller radius than single Bi2MoO6. This indicates that the BMO/LCQDs-7 photocatalyst has a faster charge transfer rate and a higher carrier separation rate than other catalysts. A Mott-Schottky (MS) curve was used to measure the flat band charge potential of pure Bi2MoO6 and BMO/LCQDs composites of different proportions in Fig. 6b. Based on the positive slope of the MS curve, it is shown that Bi2MoO6 and its corresponding composites are both n-type semiconductor materials [63]. The flat band potentials of Bi2MoO6, BMO/LCQDs-4, BMO/LCQDs-7, and BMO/LCQDs-10 are −0.79, −0.65, −0.45, and −0.59 eV, respectively. For n-type semiconductors, the flat band potential is typically 0.10–0.30 eV higher than the conduction band's (CB) potential [64]. According to the formula

, The valence band (VB) potentials for Bi2MoO6, BMO/LCQDs-4, BMO/LCQDs-7, and BMO/LCQDs-10 were 2.23, 2.24, 2.18, and 2.20 eV, respectively (vs. NHE at pH=7).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the elemental composition, valence structure, and electronic density of states of BMO/LCQDs-7. The C 1 s peak at 284.80 eV was used as a reference and corrected [65]. In Fig. 7a, in agreement with the results detected by EDS mapping, signal peaks of elements Bi, O, Mo and C were detected in the samples. In Fig. 7b, the C 1 s spectrum shows that the central peak at 284.80 eV mainly comprises graphitic carbon (C-C/CC). Meanwhile, the two signal peaks at 286.41 eV and 288.71 eV correspond to C-O and O-CO, respectively. The Bi 4 f spectrum (Fig. 7c) shows two distinct signal peaks at 156.62 and 161.34 eV attributed to Bi3+ for Bi 4 f7/2 and Bi 4 f5/2 [60,66]. In Fig. 7d, the binding energy of Mo 3d corresponds to the signal peaks at 229.51 and 232.69 eV, respectively. Based on the spin-orbit splitting of the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 states, the oxidation state of the Mo ion in Bi2MoO6 is Mo6+ [67,68]. The high-resolution O 1 s spectra shown in Fig. 7e indicate different chemical states of oxygen through the binding energies of 527.02, 529.36 and 530.86 eV [51]. The signal peak at 530.86 eV can be attributed to H2O and O2 adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst. while the peaks at 527.02 and 529.36 eV correspond to lattice oxygens in the [Bi2O2]2+ and [MoO4]2- layers in Bi2MoO6 [69]. Whereas the characteristic peaks with higher binding energies may represent more active surface reactive oxygen species, BMO/LCQDs-7 is activated upon irradiation with visible light to produce more ·O2
- , resulting in redox reactions [70]. In addition, LCQDs are mainly composed of elemental C, and it is challenging to quantify elemental C in composites by XPS analysis [71]. However, it is undeniable that BMO/LCQDs-7 composites were successfully synthesized.

In this experiment, the photocatalytic performance of the obtained samples was evaluated by performing TCH degradation experiments in a photocatalytic reactor with a condensate unit. The adsorption and photocatalytic properties of LCQDs, Bi2MoO6, BMO/LCQDs-4, BMO/LCQDs-7, and BMO/LCQDs-10 ([dosage]= 0.2 g/L) were tested for TCH (20 mg/L), respectively. Before the light reaction, a 30 min dark reaction was required to reach the adsorption equilibrium. In Fig. 8a, after 120 min of visible light irradiation, their removal rates of TCH were in the order of BMO/LCQDs-7> BMO/LCQDs-10> BMO/LCQDs-4> Bi2MoO6> LCQDs (UV−vis absorption spectra in Fig. S3). Among them, the BMO/LCQDs-7 composite had the best degradation rate of TCH, with 98.76% removal of TCH from the solution after 120 min of light exposure. However, in the absence of the photocatalyst, the concentration of TCH in the solution hardly changed with the reaction, which indicated that light alone could not remove TCH from the solution. Fig. 8a showed that the photocatalytic efficiencies of the composites doped with LCQDs were all better than those of the single Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst. This is mainly attributed to the upconversion luminescence property of LCQDs, which can absorb longer wavelengths of light and emit shorter wavelengths of light, thus stimulating the formation of electron-hole pairs on the surface of Bi2MoO6, promoting charge transfer and enhancing the photocatalytic degradation rate. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic TCH removal increased and decreased with the increasing doping amount of LCQDs. Among them, the best photocatalytic degradation rate was observed for BMO/LCQDs-7, indicating an optimal range for the ratio of LCQDs to Bi2MoO6. The main reason for this phenomenon is that the LCQDs act as charge carriers in the photocatalytic reaction system and capture the electrons emitted from the surface of Bi2MoO6 due to visible light irradiation, which hinders the electron-hole complexation and improves the photocatalytic activity. But when the LCQDs capture the electrons, the remaining overloaded LCQDs then occupy the active sites on the surface of Bi2MoO6, affecting the visible light absorption of Bi2MoO6. Meanwhile, the excess LCQDs will agglomerate with each other and form charge recombination centres to hinder charge transfer, thus affecting the photocatalytic activity of the composites. This is consistent with the previously reported phenomenon [72].

To further differentiate the photocatalytic activity of the several samples mentioned above, first-order kinetics was followed, and the following equation can express the first-order kinetic model Eq (C.1):
(C.1)
−
ln (
C
t
/
C
0
) = K t

C 0 is the initial concentration of TCH solution when adsorption equilibrium is reached, C t is the instantaneous concentration at time t of the reaction, K is the first-order kinetic rate constant, and t is the reaction time for photocatalytic degradation of TCH solution. In this case, the K value is related to environmental factors such as reaction temperature and pH. In Fig. 8b, BMO/LCQDs-7 calculated the highest K value of 0.026 min−1, which was 16.99, 2.05, 1.73, and 1.57 times higher than that of single LCQDs, Bi2MoO6, BMO/LCQDs-4, and BMO/LCQDs-10, respectively. A comprehensive comparison with previously reported photocatalysts for TCH degradation is provided in Table S1, and it is demonstrated that the BMO/LCQDs composite exhibited a higher rate of TCH degradation than the reported semiconductor.

In Fig. 9a, different removal rates of TCH (20 mg/L) were achieved with different dosages of catalysts. When the dosage of BMO/LCQDs-7 photocatalyst was 0.20 g/L, 0.40 g/L, 0.60 g/L, 0.80 g/L, and 1.00 g/L, the corresponding degradation rates of TCH after 120 min of visible-light irradiation were classified as 98.76%, 90.46%, 91.23%, 92.12%, and 82.84% (UV−vis absorption spectra in Fig. S4). Experimentally, more catalyst is not always better. The best TCH removal was achieved when the catalyst dosage was 0.20 g/L. Mainly because as the dosage of the catalyst increased, the turbidity of the solution also increased, which dispersed the visible light and reduced the transmittance [73]. In addition, excessive photocatalyst deposition in solution inhibits the production of active substances during the reaction [74]. Combining the removal rate of the prepared catalysts and the economic practicability. Therefore, the optimal dosage of 0.20 g/L of BMO/LCQDs-7 photocatalyst was selected for the subsequent photocatalytic degradation experiments, and a series of comparative experiments were carried out with this catalyst. To investigate the effect of the initial concentration of TCH antibiotics on the degradation performance of BMO/LCQDs-7 catalyst. The 20 mg/L, 50 mg/L, and 100 mg/L TCH solutions were weighed to simulate the waste solutions to be tested. In Fig. 9b, after the initial concentration of TCH increased from 20 mg/L to 100 mg/L. The adsorption of TCH by BMO/LCQDs-7 reached an equilibrium, excessive TCH molecules aggregated to the surface of the catalyst, which prevented the catalyst from being excited by the light. Ultimately, it resulted in a decrease in ·O2 - production and a decrease in TCH removal rate from 98.76% to 27.46% (UV−vis absorption spectra in Fig. S5). China is the world's largest antibiotic producer and user [75]. The survey shows that the highest concentration of TCH detected in wastewater from the medical industry in China is about 26.22 μg/L [76]. Lower saturation than BMO/LCQDs-7 photocatalytic removal of TCH, So the catalytic material prepared meets the specific practical needs.

As shown in Fig. 9c, the effect of pH on the photocatalytic TCH removal rate, which affects the surface charge of catalysts in the solution (UV−vis absorption spectra in Fig. S6). Under different pH conditions, the morphology and types of active radicals produced in the photocatalytic reaction differ. In this study, 0.1 mmol/L NaOH and 0.1 mmol/L HCL were used to control the pH of the reaction solution. In acid conditions (pH=3, pH=5), TCH in solution is mainly known as the cation H3TC+. With the increase of pH, TCH gradually changes from a positively charged ionic structure to an amphoteric one. As the pH exceeded 7, anionic TCH- structures gradually appeared. Experiments revealed that acidic conditions were more favorable for the photocatalytic removal of TCH from wastewater, but the effect was insignificant. Primarily because, under acidic conditions, a large amount of H+ within the system reacts with ·O2 - to form H2O2. As H2O2 strongly oxidizes, it oxidizes the TCH in solution to other small molecules. Furthermore, a large amount of OH- in alkaline conditions reacts with H+ in solution. Meanwhile, the electrostatic repulsion between BMO/LCQDs and TCH reduced the photocatalytic degradation rate [48,77].

The stability and recyclability of photocatalysts are essential for practical applications. In this study, under visible light exposure, TCH was subjected to five cycles of degradation experiments using the BMO/LCQDs-7 composite catalyst, and the TCH removal rates were compared (UV−vis absorption spectra in Fig. S7). After each cycle, the photocatalysts were recovered by centrifugation and washed more than three times with deionised water to remove surface adsorbed impurities. In Fig. 10a, BMO/LCQDs-7 maintained good photocatalytic activity in five successive photocatalytic reactions. The removal of TCH by the BMO/LCQDs-7 material reached 98.76% in the first reaction. After the first cycle, the TCH removal rate decreased to 92.77%, which was 5.99% lower than the first reaction, mainly because the TCH molecules occupied the active sites on the surface of the BMO/LCQDs-7 composite photocatalysts, and the simple water and alcohol washes could not completely remove all the pollutants on the surface of the catalysts, which made the adsorption capacity of the BMO/LCQDs-7 composite photocatalysts decreased. After the second cycle, the dark reaction adsorption changes were not obvious compared with the first cycle, mainly because the adsorption equilibrium had been completely reached on the catalyst surface, and the TCH removal rate could still be maintained at 89.07%, which showed good recyclability. The slight decrease in photocatalytic activity after five consecutive cycles could be attributed to the unavoidable quality loss of the photocatalyst during separation and recycling as well as the photobleaching of the photocatalyst surface. In Fig. 10b, the diffraction peaks in the XRD spectra of BMO/LCQDs-7 before and after recycling did not show significant differences, which proves that five cycles did not change the BMO/ LCQDs-7 crystal structure, so it indicates that the prepared BMO/LCQDs-7 composites have high stability. In addition, in Fig. 10c-d, SEM and TEM images showed that the morphology and structure of BMO/LCQDs-7 hardly changed significantly after cycling compared with the sample before the reaction, and the LCQDs were visible, which indicated that the composites had a high structural stability, and had a promising application in practical industrial production. However, it was found that compared with 0.20 g of catalyst weighed before the cycling experiment, only 0.05 g of photocatalyst was left after five cycles. The problems of catalyst loss and the low recovery rate are yet to be further improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of TCH mechanism and pathway

ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the active compounds involved in the photocatalytic degradation of TCH, capture experiments were conducted with the aid of three free radical scavengers added to the reaction system. Quenching experiments were conducted using EDTA, IPA, and BQ to quench hole (h+), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and superoxide radical (·O2
-
), respectively [78–80]. In Fig. 11a, after adding EDTA and BQ, the results indicate that BMO/LCQDs-7 significantly reduces the photocatalytic degradation rate of TCH, with inhibitions of 60.24% and 81.15%, respectively. It is indicated that h+ and ·O2
- have a significant role in TCH degradation and are the primary active substances that promote TCH degradation. Among them, in Eq. (D.1), BQ is converted to BQ·- by a reductive reaction with ·O2
- . Subsequently, in Eqs. (D.2)-(D.4), BQ·- continues to scavenge free radicals by interacting with other free radicals. Thus, BQ can trap ·O2
- and convert it into harmless products, processes can be described by the following Eqs: (D.1)-(D.4).
(D.1) BQ+·O2
- →BQ·-+O2
(D.2) BQ·-+H + →HQ·Q
(D.3) HQ·Q+·O2
- →HQ-+O2
(D.4) HQ-+H + →H2O

After adding IPA, the degradation rate of TCH decreased significantly, inhibiting only 5.03%. This suggests that a small amount of ·OH also participated in the degradation of TCH. Electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR) experiments further elucidated the production of active substances during the BMO/LCQDs-7 catalysis. In Fig. 11b-c, EPR signals were detected in the presence of 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) with significant ·O2
- and ·OH characteristic peaks. This indicates that it has excellent photo-oxidative reduction capability [81,82].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic mechanism of LCQDs doped Bi2MoO6
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the above discussion, a photocatalytic TCH degradation mechanism for LCQDs/Bi2MoO6 hydrangea-like composites was proposed. In Fig. 12, when visible light is irradiated onto the surface of the LCQDs/Bi2MoO6 hydrangea, the electrons on the VB energy level of Bi2MoO6 are excited to form negatively charged electrons (e-) that jump to the CB energy level of Bi2MoO6. In contrast, positively charged photogenerated h+ continue to be retained at the VB energy level, resulting in the formation of the photogenerated e--h+ pairs (Eq. (E.1)), causing the generation of redox potential on the microsphere surface [10,83]. Due to the excellent conductive properties of LCQDs, photogenerated electrons at the Bi2MoO6 CB energy level are preferentially transferred to LCQDs and accumulate on their surface [70]. The conjugated π-structure of LCQDs additionally acts as an effective transporter so that the electrons aggregated on its surface effectively capture the adsorbed O2 on the surface of Bi2MoO6, converting it into a ·O2
- with oxidizing solid properties (Eq. (E.2)). Meanwhile, the photogenerated cavities will react with H2O adsorbed on the surface to form ·OH and H+ (Eq. (E.5)) [84]. TCH is eventually degraded into other small molecules in the presence of ·O2
- , ·OH, and h+.

Based on the above analysis, the LCQDs/Bi2MoO6 photocatalytic process can be illustrated by the following Eqs: (E.1)-(E.6).
(E.1) Bi2MoO6+hv→e-+h+
(E.2) LCQDs+e-+O2→·O2 -
(E.3) ·O2 -+2H++2e-→H2O2
(E.4) H2O2+e-→·OH+OH-
(E.5) h++H2O→·OH+H+
(E.6) (h+,·O2 -,·OH)+TCH→degradation products

To investigate the degradation pathway of tetracycline hydrochloride, we identified TCH degradation derivatives through LC-MS testing (Fig. S8). Fig. 13 illustrates the two potential pathways for TCH degradation. In pathway 1, free HCl molecules are dispersed in the solvent. TCH (m/z=445) is converted to TC, and the methyl group is removed in the presence of ·O2
- to produce P1–1 (m/z=430). As the photocatalytic reaction proceeds, the methyl group on the organic ring is removed to produce P1–2 (m/z=417). In addition, the reaction destroys the carbon ring in the presence of reactive radicals to produce P1–3 (m/z=362), which is ring-opened to produce P3 as the response continues (m/z=274) [53]. In pathway 2, TCH (m/z=445) is attacked by OH- to generate intermediates P2–1 (m/z=462) and P2–2 (m/z=477) [85]. P2–2 (m/z=477) was demethylated to produce P2–3 (m/z=459). Considering the low binding energies of the C-N and C-C bonds, a new product, P2–4 (m/z = 342), was generated by N-demethylation and deamidation processes and P3 (m/z = 274) by a ring-opening process [86]. Subsequently, after further photodecomposition, the hydrocarbon ring was opened to generate a series of small molecule intermediates, such as P3–1 (m/z=129) and P3–2 (m/z=102). After further oxidation, these intermediates were finally mineralized into inorganic ions such as CO2 and H2O to achieve the photocatalytic degradation of TCH [87].

Conclusively, this study successfully prepared LCQDs/Bi2MoO6 hydrangea-like composite photocatalysts using a solvothermal process. And the obtained BMO/LCQDs-7 photocatalysts exhibited excellent photodegradation rates for TCH under visible light irradiation. The doping of LCQDs significantly enhanced the photocatalytic activity of Bi2MoO6, which was attributed to the fact that the moderate amount of LCQDs promoted the photogenerated carrier separation and migration within the system and hindered the electron-hole pair complexation. UV–vis DRS confirmed the extension of BMO/LCQDs-7 photoresponse to the visible region. Free radical trapping experiments and EPR spectra further demonstrated that h+ and ·O2
- were the main active substances in the photocatalytic process. Meanwhile, five-cycle experiments demonstrated that the BMO/LCQDs-7 hydrangea-like composite photocatalyst has good stability and recyclability, avoiding the secondary pollution caused by the catalyst. In future research, the problem of low recovery of BMO/LCQDs-7 catalysts after cycling should not be ignored. This study demonstrates that LCQDs/Bi2MoO6 hydrangea-like composite photocatalysts are promising for new energy sources and remediation of water environments.
